content: Earth’s seasons arise primarily from the obliquity of its rotational axis—approximately 23.44° relative to the orbital plane—rather than orbital proximity to the Sun. This axial tilt causes the solar declination to oscillate between ±23.44° over the year, modulating the angle and duration of insolation at different latitudes. Consequently, hemispheres alternately experience variations in solar zenith angle and day length, driving the pronounced seasonal contrasts in temperature and climate. While Earth’s orbital eccentricity (~0.0167) introduces minor asymmetries in insolation, its effect is secondary compared to obliquity. Moreover, axial precession (~26,000-year cycle) and variations in tilt amplitude contribute to long-term climatic modulations known as Milankovitch cycles, influencing glacial-interglacial patterns rather than annual seasons. Thus, the seasons embody a dynamic interplay between Earth’s stable axial tilt and orbital parameters, with axial orientation dictating the latitudinal distribution of solar energy and underpinning the rhythmic environmental shifts that define seasonal change.
